idea.hook: You think galaxies are the biggest cosmic structures?
facts: Our Milky Way galaxy is ~100,000 light-years across.
Our Local Group of galaxies (including Andromeda) spans ~10 million light-years.
The Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall, a filament of galaxy clusters, stretches an estimated 10 BILLION light-years.
idea.payoff: This single structure is nearly 1/9th the diameter of the entire OBSERVABLE UNIVERSE (approx. 93 billion light-years)! It's mind-bogglingly huge.
idea.title: Cosmic Wall: UNIMAGINABLY HUGE! 🤯🌌

scenes.text: You think galaxies are the biggest cosmic structures?
scenes.visual: A vast field of stars, slowly zooming out to reveal a beautiful, detailed spiral galaxy in the distance.

scenes.text: Our Milky Way galaxy: ~100,000 LY across.
scenes.visual: An animated rendering of the Milky Way galaxy seen from above, with a measurement bar appearing across its diameter.

scenes.text: Our Local Group of galaxies: ~10 MILLION LY.
scenes.visual: Zoom out further to show the Milky Way and Andromeda galaxies, plus other smaller galaxies, depicted as bright nodes within a conceptual, expanding sphere.

scenes.text: Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall: ~10 BILLION LY.
scenes.visual: A dramatic, rapid zoom out. The Local Group becomes a tiny speck as a vast, glowing filamentary structure of galaxy clusters (cosmic web) stretches across the screen, highlighting the Great Wall section.

scenes.text: It's ~1/9th the ENTIRE Observable Universe! (93 Billion LY)
scenes.visual: The entire Observable Universe represented as a shimmering, expanding sphere, with the Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall visually scaled and highlighted as a significant arc within it. The background subtly pulsates with cosmic energy.
